name: Bones of the Human Body
description: The major named bones of the human skeleton, from the skull and spine to the hands and feet. Read the description, type the bone; extras add location, landmarks, and how many bones make up each region.

fields.front: The bone that forms the forehead and the upper rim of the eye sockets.
fields.extra: One of the eight cranial bones that enclose the brain.

fields.front: The paired bones forming most of the roof and upper sides of the skull.
fields.extra: They meet at the top of the head along the sagittal suture.

fields.front: The paired bones at the lower sides of the skull that house the middle and inner ear.
fields.extra: The mastoid process behind the ear is part of this bone.

fields.front: The bone at the back and base of the skull, pierced by the hole for the spinal cord.
fields.extra: That hole is the foramen magnum.

fields.front: The butterfly-shaped bone spanning the base of the skull that cradles the pituitary gland.
fields.extra: It touches every other cranial bone.

fields.front: The light, spongy bone between the eyes that forms part of the nasal cavity and eye sockets.
fields.extra: Its cribriform plate lets the olfactory nerves through.

fields.front: The lower jaw, and the only bone of the skull that moves.
fields.extra: The strongest and largest bone of the face.

fields.front: The paired bones of the upper jaw that also form the front of the hard palate.
fields.extra: They hold the upper teeth.

fields.front: The cheekbone.
fields.extra: It forms the prominence of the cheek and part of the eye socket.

fields.front: The paired bones that form the bridge of the nose.
fields.extra: The lower part of the nose is cartilage, not bone.

fields.front: The smallest bones of the face, in the inner corner of each eye socket.
fields.extra: They form part of the tear duct channel.

fields.front: The thin flat bone that forms the lower part of the nasal septum.
fields.extra: Its name means 'plowshare' from its shape.

fields.front: The 'hammer', the outermost of the three middle-ear ossicles.
fields.extra: It is attached to the eardrum.

fields.front: The 'anvil', the middle of the three tiny ear bones.
fields.extra: It sits between the malleus and the stapes.

fields.front: The 'stirrup' of the middle ear, and the smallest bone in the human body.
fields.extra: About 3 mm long; it passes vibrations to the inner ear.

fields.front: The U-shaped throat bone that anchors the tongue and is the only bone not joined to any other bone.
fields.extra: It floats, held by muscles and ligaments in the neck.

fields.front: The first cervical vertebra (C1), which holds up the skull.
fields.extra: Named for the Titan who held up the sky; it lets you nod 'yes'.

fields.front: The second cervical vertebra (C2), whose peg lets the head rotate.
fields.extra: Its peg (the dens) lets you shake your head 'no'.

fields.front: The triangular bone of five fused vertebrae at the base of the spine.
fields.extra: It wedges into the pelvis between the two hip bones.

fields.front: The tailbone at the very bottom of the spine.
fields.extra: Three to five tiny fused vertebrae; the remnant of a tail.

fields.front: The flat breastbone at the front of the chest.
fields.extra: The ribs and collarbones attach to it.

fields.front: Each curved bone of the chest cage (there are 12 pairs).
fields.extra: They protect the heart and lungs.

fields.front: The collarbone.
fields.extra: The most commonly broken bone in the body.

fields.front: The shoulder blade.
fields.extra: A flat triangular bone that the arm and collarbone attach to.

fields.front: The single long bone of the upper arm.
fields.extra: Its lower end forms the elbow with the radius and ulna.

fields.front: The forearm bone on the thumb side.
fields.extra: It rotates around the ulna when you turn your palm.

fields.front: The forearm bone on the little-finger side that forms the point of the elbow.
fields.extra: The bump of your elbow is its olecranon.

fields.front: The eight small bones of the wrist (their collective name).
fields.extra: Arranged in two rows of four.

fields.front: The boat-shaped wrist bone most often broken in a fall onto an outstretched hand.
fields.extra: A common and easily missed fracture.

fields.front: The five bones of the palm of the hand.
fields.extra: Each connects a wrist bone to a finger.

fields.front: The bones of the fingers and toes.
fields.extra: Fingers and most toes have three; the thumb and big toe have two.

fields.front: The large flared upper part of the hip bone (where you rest your hands on your hips).
fields.extra: The broadest part of the pelvis.

fields.front: The lower rear part of the hip bone that you sit on.
fields.extra: Its rounded base takes your weight when seated.

fields.front: The front part of the hip bone, where the two halves meet.
fields.extra: The two pubic bones join at the pubic symphysis.

fields.front: The thigh bone, and the longest and strongest bone in the body.
fields.extra: It bears the body's weight and can support many times it.

fields.front: The kneecap.
fields.extra: A bone embedded in the tendon of the thigh muscle; it protects the knee joint.

fields.front: The shinbone, the larger and inner of the two lower-leg bones.
fields.extra: It bears nearly all the weight below the knee.

fields.front: The thin outer bone of the lower leg.
fields.extra: It carries little weight but anchors muscles and stabilizes the ankle.

fields.front: The seven bones of the ankle and heel (their collective name).
fields.extra: They form the back of the foot and the arch.

fields.front: The ankle bone that sits between the leg bones and the heel.
fields.extra: It transfers the body's weight to the foot.

fields.front: The heel bone, and the largest of the ankle bones.
fields.extra: The Achilles tendon attaches to it.

fields.front: The five long bones that form the arch of the foot.
fields.extra: They connect the ankle bones to the toes.

fields.front: How many bones are in the typical adult human body?
fields.extra: Babies are born with about 300; many fuse together during growth.

fields.front: How many vertebrae make up the adult spine, counting the fused ones?
fields.extra: 7 neck, 12 chest, 5 lower back, 5 fused in the sacrum, and 4 fused in the coccyx.

fields.front: How many cervical (neck) vertebrae do humans have?
fields.extra: Nearly all mammals have seven, from mice to giraffes.

fields.front: How many pairs of ribs does a typical human have?
fields.extra: 7 'true' pairs reach the sternum, 3 'false' pairs join by cartilage, and 2 'floating' pairs do not attach at the front.
